question.text: Scenario: Meera Srinivasan is the compliance officer at Galaxy Research Services, a SEBI-registered research entity. She discovers the following situations during her quarterly review: (1) Analyst Rajan published a Buy report on PharmaTech Ltd and his wife purchased PharmaTech shares 15 days before the report was published. (2) The firm received Rs.50 lakh from InfoSys Corp for investment banking advisory services 6 months ago. Analyst Priya, who covers InfoSys Corp, did not disclose this in her latest research report. (3) Analyst Vikram's compensation for the year was partly determined based on the revenue his research helped generate from brokerage services for specific clients. (4) The research team is located on the same floor as the investment banking team, sharing common meeting rooms and a common server for document storage. (5) Analyst Deepa received a call from the CEO of MicroChip Ltd sharing that the company will announce a major acquisition next week. This information has not been made public. Question 2 of 5: Priya's failure to disclose the Rs.50 lakh IB fee from InfoSys Corp violates:

answer.text: No regulation since the payment was 6 months ago
Regulation 19(ii) which requires disclosure of compensation from subject company received in the past 12 months
Only internal policy, not SEBI regulations
The regulation only applies if the payment exceeds Rs.1 crore

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Regulation 19(ii)(d): Must disclose whether entity/associates received compensation from subject company in past 12 months. 6 months is within the look-back period. **The Trap:** 12-month look-back covers the Rs.50 lakh payment received 6 months ago. There is no minimum threshold for disclosure. **Cross-Exam Context:** Ch 14 NISM XV. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Maintain a database of all compensation received from potential subject companies. Cross-reference before publishing any research report.
